name: Elm3d.Angle

values.name: lerp
values.comment:
Linearly interpolate from one angle to another, without issues when
crossing across the `2π` to `0` boundary.

    lerp { from = 0, to = pi, step = 0 } == 0

    lerp { from = 0, to = pi, step = 0.5 } == pi / 2

    lerp { from = 0, to = pi, step = 1 } == pi

    lerp { from = 2 * pi, to = pi / 2, step = 0.5 } == pi / 4

This is a nice way to change something's rotation on each frame when working with `Elm3d.Node.withOnFrame`.

    cameraSpin : Float
    cameraSpin =
        Elm3d.Float.lerp
            { from = model.cameraSpin
            , to = model.playerRotation
            , step = dt * 10.0
            }

**Not working with angles?** Use [Elm3d.Float.lerp](./Elm3d-Float#lerp) instead! It won't be limited to values ranging from `0` to `2π`
values.type: { from : Basics.Float, to : Basics.Float, step : Basics.Float } -> Basics.Float

name: Elm3d.Camera

aliases.name: Camera
aliases.comment: Cameras allow you to control how users see your 3D world.

values.name: isometric
values.comment:
A specialized orthographic camera designed for making isometric 3D scenes.

    import Elm3d.Vector2

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        isometric
            { size = 10
            , incline = pi / 4
            , spin = pi / 4
            , distance = 50
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            , offset = Elm3d.Vector2.zero
            }

  - **`size`** - How many units wide this camera can see (height is calculated by viewport ratio)
  - **`range`** - The near and far limit for visiblity (anything within range of the camera will be visible)
  - **`incline`** - The vertical angle for the camera, in radians
  - **`spin`** - The horizontal angle for the camera, in radians
  - **`distance`** - How far the camera is from the view target (easier than working with position)
  - **`offset`** - The XZ position of the camera (easier than working with position)
values.type: { size : Basics.Float, incline : Basics.Float, spin : Basics.Float, distance : Basics.Float, range : ( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ), offset : Elm3d.Vector2.Vector2 } -> Elm3d.Camera.Camera msg

values.name: map
values.comment:
Convert a camera with one `msg` type into another. This is commonly needed when nesting
the Elm Architecture with a page or component:

    import Pages.Dashboard

    type Msg
        = Dashboard Pages.Dashboard.Msg

    camera : Camera Pages.Dashboard.Msg
    camera =
        ...

    newCamera : Camera Msg
    newCamera =
        map Dashboard camera

values.name: orthographic
values.comment:
Create a camera with an orthographic projection.

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }

  - **`size`** - How many units wide this camera can see (height is calculated by viewport ratio)
  - **`range`** - The near and far limit for visiblity (anything within range of the camera will be visible)
values.type: { size : Basics.Float, range : ( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) } -> Elm3d.Camera.Camera msg

values.name: perspective
values.comment:
Create a camera with a perspective projection.

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        perspective
            { fov = 60
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }

  - **`fov`** - The field-of-view for this camera, in degrees
  - **`range`** - The near and far limit for visiblity (anything within range of the camera will be visible)
values.type: { fov : Basics.Float, range : ( Basics.Float, Basics.Float ) } -> Elm3d.Camera.Camera msg

values.name: withOnFrame
values.comment:
Provide a message to call every frame.

    import Elm3d.Frame exposing (Frame)

    type Msg
        = CameraOnFrame Frame

    camera : Camera Msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withOnFrame CameraOnFrame

values.name: withOnInput
values.comment:
Provide a message to call for any input event.

    import Elm3d.Input.Event exposing (Event)

    type Msg
        = CameraOnInput Event

    camera : Camera Msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withOnInput CameraOnInput

values.name: withPosition
values.comment:
Set this camera's position using a 3D vector.

    import Elm3d.Vector3 exposing (Vector3)

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withPositionX (Elm3d.Vector3.new 0 10 10)

values.name: withPositionX
values.comment:
Set this camera's position along the **x-axis**.

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withPositionX 5

values.name: withPositionY
values.comment:
Set this camera's position along the **y-axis**.

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withPositionY 5

values.name: withPositionZ
values.comment:
Set this camera's position along the **z-axis**.

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withPositionZ 5

values.name: withRotation
values.comment:
Set this camera's rotation using a 3D vector

    import Elm3d.Vector3 exposing (Vector3)

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withRotation (Elm3d.Vector3.new 5 0 2)

values.name: withRotationX
values.comment:
Rotate the camera around the **x-axis**

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withRotationX 2

values.name: withRotationY
values.comment:
Rotate the camera around the **y-axis**

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withRotationY 2

values.name: withRotationZ
values.comment:
Rotate the camera around the **z-axis**

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        orthographic
            { size = 10
            , range = ( 1, 100 )
            }
            |> withRotationZ 2

name: Elm3d.Color

values.name: black
values.comment: black == rgb 0 0 0

values.name: blue
values.comment:
The primary blue color.

    blue == rgb 0 0 255

values.name: cyan
values.comment:
The secondary cyan color

    cyan == rgb 0 255 255

values.name: green
values.comment:
The primary green color.

    green == rgb 0 255 0

values.name: magenta
values.comment:
The secondary magenta color

    magenta == rgb 255 0 255

values.name: red
values.comment:
The primary red color.

    red == rgb 255 0 0

values.name: rgb
values.comment:
Create a color from RGB integers (0-255)

    rgb 255 255 255 == white

    rgb 0 0 0 == black

    rgb 255 0 0 == red

    rgb 0 255 0 == green

    rgb 0 0 255 == blue

values.name: rgba
values.comment:
Create a color that supports transparency

    rgba 255 255 255 1 == white

    rgba 0 0 0 1 == black

    rgba 0 0 0 0 == transparent

values.name: toHtmlColor
values.comment:
Convert a color to a string that can be used in a CSS style tag

    toHtmlColor red == "rgba(255, 0, 0, 1.0)"

    toHtmlColor magenta == "rgba(255, 0, 255, 1.0)"

    toHtmlColor black == "rgba(0, 0, 0, 1.0)"

    toHtmlColor transparent == "rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.0)"

values.name: transparent
values.comment:
Completely transparent

    transparent == rgba 0 0 0 0

values.name: white
values.comment: white == rgb 255 255 255

values.name: yellow
values.comment:
The secondary yellow color

    yellow == rgb 255 255 0

name: Elm3d.Component

unions.name: Model
unions.comment:
Tracks the state of the Elm3D component.

    -- 1️⃣ Add this to your existing `Model`

    import Elm3d.Component

    type alias Model =
        { ...
        , elm3d : Elm3d.Component.Model
        }

unions.name: Msg
unions.comment:
These messages are handled by the update function.

    -- 3️⃣ Add this to your `Msg` type

    import Elm3d.Component

    type Msg
        = ...
        | Elm3d Elm3d.Component.Msg

values.name: init
values.comment:
Initialize the Elm3D component, and fire off any initial commands.

    -- 2️⃣ Add this in your `init` function

    import Elm3d.Component
    import Elm3d.Node exposing (Node)


    init : Flags -> ( Model, Cmd Msg )
    init flags =
        let
            (elm3d, elm3dCmd) =
                Elm3d.Component.init
                    { nodes = nodes
                    }
        in
        ( { ...
          , elm3d = elm3d
          }
        , Cmd.map Elm3d elm3dCmd
        )


    nodes : List (Node Msg)
    nodes =
        ...
values.type: { nodes : List.List (Elm3d.Internals.Node.Node msg) } -> ( Elm3d.Component.Model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd Elm3d.Component.Msg )

values.name: subscriptions
values.comment:
This needs to be wired up for input events and any on frame listeners.

    -- 5️⃣ Add this in your `subscriptions` function

    import Elm3d.Component
    import Elm3d.Camera exposing (Camera)


    subscriptions : Model -> Sub Msg
    subscriptions model =
        Sub.batch
            [ ...
            , Elm3d.Component.subscriptions
                { fpsLimit = Nothing
                , camera = camera
                , nodes = nodes
                }
            ]
values.type: { fpsLimit : Maybe.Maybe Basics.Float, camera : Elm3d.Internals.Camera.Camera msg, nodes : List.List (Elm3d.Internals.Node.Node msg) } -> Elm3d.Component.Model -> Platform.Sub.Sub Elm3d.Component.Msg

values.name: update
values.comment:
Updates the Elm3d `Model`, based on the the current `Msg` coming in.

    -- 4️⃣ Add this in your `update` function

    import Elm3d.Component
    import Elm3d.Camera exposing (Camera)


    update : Msg -> Model -> ( Model, Cmd Msg )
    update msg model =
        case msg of
            ...

            Elm3d elm3dMsg ->
                Elm3d.Component.update
                    { msg = elm3dMsg
                    , model = model.elm3d
                    , toModel = \elm3d -> { model | elm3d = elm3d }
                    , toMsg = Elm3d
                    , camera = camera
                    , nodes = nodes
                    }


    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        ...
values.type: { toMsg : Elm3d.Component.Msg -> msg, toModel : Elm3d.Component.Model -> model, model : Elm3d.Component.Model, msg : Elm3d.Component.Msg, camera : Elm3d.Internals.Camera.Camera msg, nodes : List.List (Elm3d.Internals.Node.Node msg) } -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )

values.name: view
values.comment:
This renders the Elm3D view as HTML for the user to see.

    -- 6️⃣ Add this anywhere in your `view` function


    import Elm3d.Color
    import Elm3d.Component
    import Html exposing (..)

    view : Model -> Html Msg
    view model =
        div []
            [ h1 [] [ text "My app!" ]
            , Elm3d.Component.view
                { showFps = False
                , size = ( 400, 300 )
                , toMsg = Elm3d
                , background = Elm3d.Color.transparent
                , camera = camera
                , nodes = nodes
                }
            ]
values.type: { showFps : Basics.Bool, size : ( Basics.Int, Basics.Int ), toMsg : Elm3d.Component.Msg -> msg, background : Elm3d.Color.Color, nodes : List.List (Elm3d.Internals.Node.Node msg), camera : Elm3d.Internals.Camera.Camera msg } -> Elm3d.Component.Model -> Html.Html msg

name: Elm3d.Float
comment:
This module defines any convenience functions for working with floats in the context of 3D rendering. (There's just one for now!)

@docs lerp

values.name: lerp
values.comment:
Linearly interpolate from one float to another, by the given step size.

    lerp { from = 0, to = 10, step = 0 } == 0

    lerp { from = 0, to = 10, step = 0.5 } == 5

    lerp { from = 0, to = 10, step = 1 } == 10

    lerp { from = 0, to = -4, step = 0.25 } == -1

This is a nice way to animate a numeric value each frame when working with `Elm3d.Node.withOnFrame`.

    newZoom : Float
    newZoom =
        Elm3d.Float.lerp
            { from = model.zoom
            , to = model.targetZoom
            , step = dt * zoomSpeed
            }

**Working with angles?** Use [Elm3d.Angle.lerp](./Elm3d-Angle#lerp) instead! It prevents crazy stuff happening when animating across the `0` / `2π` threshold. Unless you like unpredictable 360-no-scope spins- then definitely use this lerp!
values.type: { from : Basics.Float, to : Basics.Float, step : Basics.Float } -> Basics.Float

name: Elm3d.Input.Event

unions.name: Event
unions.comment: When using `Node.onInput`, these are all the kinds of input events that you can expect.

name: Elm3d.Input.Key

unions.name: Key
unions.comment: Represents a key on your keyboard

values.name: fromCode
values.comment:
Get a `Key` from its character code.

    toCode 13 == Just KEY_ENTER

    toCode 40 == Just KEY_ARROW_DOWN

    toCode 420 == Nothing

values.name: toCode
values.comment:
Convert a `Key` to it's character code.

    toCode KEY_ENTER == 13

    toCode KEY_ARROW_DOWN == 40

name: Elm3d.Input.Mouse

unions.name: Button
unions.comment: Represents which mouse button that was pressed, released, etc

name: Elm3d.Isometric

values.name: toInputVector
values.comment:
Depending on the angle of your isometric camera, a player's directional input (WASD or arrow keys) might not map to up/down/left/right on the screen.

This function takes in the horizontal angle your isometric camera is using to rotate the players input by the correct amount

    import Elm3d.Camera
    import Elm3d.Frame exposing (Frame)

    cameraSpin : Float
    cameraSpin =
        pi / 4

    camera : Camera msg
    camera =
        Elm3d.Camera.isometric
            { ...
            , spin = cameraSpin
            }

    toMovementDelta : Frame -> Vector2
    toMovementDelta frame =
        toInputVector
            { spin = cameraSpin
            , input =
                Elm3d.Frame.toInputVector frame
                    { x = ( KEY_ARROW_LEFT, KEY_ARROW_RIGHT )
                    , y = ( KEY_ARROW_DOWN, KEY_ARROW_UP )
                    }
            }

name: Elm3d.Node

aliases.name: Node
aliases.comment: A node is something that can be rendered by an Elm3d program

values.name: block
values.comment:
Create a rectangular block with the specified side lengths and color.

    import Elm3d.Color
    import Elm3d.Vector3

    blueBlock : Node msg
    blueBlock =
        block
            { size = Elm3d.Vector3.new 1 2 4
            , color = Elm3d.Color.blue
            }
values.type: { size : Elm3d.Vector3.Vector3, color : Elm3d.Color.Color } -> Elm3d.Node.Node msg

values.name: cube
values.comment:
Create a cube with the given size and color.

    import Elm3d.Color

    redCube : Node msg
    redCube =
        cube
            { size = 1
            , color = Elm3d.Color.red
            }
values.type: { size : Basics.Float, color : Elm3d.Color.Color } -> Elm3d.Node.Node msg

values.name: group
map
values.comment:
Convert a node with one `msg` type into another. This is commonly needed when nesting
the Elm Architecture with a page or component:

    import Pages.Dashboard

    type Msg
        = Dashboard Pages.Dashboard.Msg

    node : Node Pages.Dashboard.Msg
    node =
        ...

    newNode : Node Msg
    newNode =
        map Dashboard node

values.name: obj
values.comment:
Create a 3D model from an OBJ file.

    tavern : Node msg
    tavern =
        obj { url = "/assets/tavern.obj" }

When used with an `Elm3d.Program`, this will _automatically
fetch_ any required `.mtl` and `.png` textures via HTTP requests.

This is an **incomplete implementation** of [the OBJ spec](https://paulbourke.net/dataformats/obj/).
It doesn't cover all aspects of rendering materials. It was initially designed to render the assets from this [KayKit 3D asset pack](https://kaylousberg.itch.io/kaykit-medieval-hexagon).

values.name: withOnFrame
values.comment:
Provide a message to call every frame.

    import Elm3d.Frame exposing (Frame)

    type Msg
        = TavernOnFrame Frame

    myCube : Node Msg
    myCube =
        cube { size = 1, color = red }
            |> withOnFrame TavernOnFrame

values.name: withOnInput
values.comment:
Provide a message to call for any input event.

    import Elm3d.Input.Event exposing (Event)

    type Msg
        = TavernOnInput Event

    myCube : Node Msg
    myCube =
        cube { size = 1, color = red }
            |> withOnInput TavernOnInput

values.name: withPosition
values.comment:
Set this node's position using a 3D vector.

    import Elm3d.Vector3 exposing (Vector3)

    position : Vector3
    position =
        Elm3d.Vector3.new 5 2 -1

    myCube : Node msg
    myCube =
        cube { size = 1, color = red }
            |> withPosition position

values.name: withPositionX
withPositionY
withPositionZ
withRotation
withRotationX
withRotationY
withRotationZ
withScale
withScaleX
withScaleY
withScaleZ

name: Elm3d.Program

aliases.name: Program
aliases.comment: A program for creating 3D games and web applications

aliases.name: View
aliases.comment: This value is returned by your `view` function.
aliases.type: { viewport : Elm3d.Viewport.Viewport, background : Elm3d.Color.Color, camera : Elm3d.Camera.Camera msg, nodes : List.List (Elm3d.Internals.Node.Node msg) }

values.name: element
values.comment:
Create an Elm3D program that can track state, send
side effects, and subscribe to events.

    main : Program Flags Model Msg
    main =
        Elm3d.Program.element
            { init = init
            , update = update
            , subscriptions = subscriptions
            , view = view
            }

    {-|

        type alias Flags =
            ...

        type alias Model =
            ...

        init : Flags -> ( Model, Cmd Msg )
        init flags =
            ...

        type Msg
            = ...

        update : Msg -> Model -> ( Model, Cmd Msg )
        update msg model =
            ...

        subscriptions : Model -> Sub Msg
        subscriptions model =
            ...

        view : Model -> Elm3d.Program.View Msg
        view model =
            ...

    -}
values.type: { init : flags -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), update : msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ), subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, view : model -> Elm3d.Program.View msg } -> Elm3d.Program.Program flags model msg

values.name: sandbox
values.comment:
Create an Elm3D program that can track state.

    main : Program () Model Msg
    main =
        Elm3d.Program.sandbox
            { init = init
            , update = update
            , view = view
            }

    {-|

        type alias Model =
            ...

        init : Model
        init =
            ...

        type Msg
            = ...

        update : Msg -> Model -> Model
        update msg model =
            ...

        view : Model -> Elm3d.Program.View Msg
        view model =
            ...

    -}
values.type: { init : flags -> model, update : msg -> model -> model, view : model -> Elm3d.Program.View msg } -> Elm3d.Program.Program flags model msg

values.name: view
values.comment:
Create a stateless Elm3D program from a view record.

    main : Program () () ()
    main =
        Elm3d.Program.view
            { viewport = Elm3d.Viewport.fullscreen
            , background = Elm3d.Color.white
            , camera =
                Elm3d.Camera.perspective
                    { fov = 60
                    , range = ( 0.01, 1000 )
                    }
            , nodes =
                [ Node.cube
                    { size = 2
                    , color = Elm3d.Color.blue
                    }
                ]
            }

name: Elm3d.Vector2

values.name: add
values.comment:
Add two vectors together

    add (new 1 0) (new 2 1) == new 3 1

    add zero one == one

values.name: clamp
values.comment:
Clamp a 2D vector between a min and max vector

    original = new 10 -50

    clamped =
        original
            |> clamp (new 0 0) (new 5 5)

    clamped == new 5 0

values.name: direction
values.comment:
Get the direction from one vector to another.

    playerPosition : Vector2
    playerPosition =
        new 4 3

    enemyPosition : Vector2
    enemyPosition =
        new 2 0

    playerToEnemy : Vector2
    playerToEnemy =
        direction
            { from = playerPosition
            , to = enemyPosition
            }

**Fun fact**: This is the same as _subtracting_ one vector from the other!

values.name: fromFloat
fromRecord
values.comment:
Create a vector from a record

    position : Vector2
    position =
        fromRecord { x = 3, y = 4 }

values.name: length
values.comment:
Get the length of the current vector

    length (new 1 0) == 1

    length (new 3 4) == 5

values.name: multiply
values.comment:
Multiply one vector by another. The resulting vector has the value `(x1 * x2, y1 * y2)`

    multiply (new 3 4) (new 1 1) == new 3 4

    multiply (new 1 1) (new 3 4) == new 3 4

    multiply (new 3 4) (new 1 0) == new 3 0

    multiply (new 3 4) (new 2 0) == new 6 0

    multiply zero one == zero

values.name: negativeX
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (-1, 0)

    negativeX == fromRecord { x = -1, y = 0 }

    negativeX == new -1 0

    negativeX == scale -1 positiveX

values.name: negativeY
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, -1)

    negativeY == fromRecord { x = 0, y = -1 }

    negativeY == new 0 -1

    negativeY == scale -1 positiveY

values.name: new
values.comment:
Create a vector from two floats

    position : Vector2
    position =
        new 3 4

values.name: normalize
values.comment:
A unit vector with the same direction as the given vector.

    normalize (new 1 0) == new 1 0

    normalize (new 2 0) == new 1 0

    normalize (new 1 1) == new (sqrt 0.5) (sqrt 0.5)

    normalize (new 10 10) == new (sqrt 0.5) (sqrt 0.5)

For convenience, normalizing a vector of length zero, returns the input vector:

    normalize (new 0 0) == new 0 0

values.name: one
values.comment:
Create the vector (1, 1)

    one == fromRecord { x = 1, y = 1 }

    one == new 1 1

    one == fromFloat 1

values.name: positiveX
positiveY
rotate
values.comment:
Rotate a vector by the given angle

    rotate pi positiveX == negativeX

    rotate (pi / 2) positiveX == positiveY

values.name: scale
values.comment:
Multiply the vector by a scalar value.

    scale 2 (new 1 1) == new 2 2

    scale 5 (new 1 1) == new 5 5

    scale 5 (new 2 0) == new 10 0

    scale 5 (new 3 4) == multiply (new 5 5) (new 3 4)

    scale 0 one == zero

values.name: toRecord
values.comment: Get the vector as an Elm record

values.name: x
values.comment: Get the x-coordinate for this vector

values.name: y
values.comment: Get the y-coordinate for this vector

values.name: zero
values.comment:
Create the vector (0, 0)

    zero == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0 }

    zero == new 0 0

    zero == fromFloat 0

name: Elm3d.Vector3

values.name: add
values.comment:
Add two vectors together

    add (new 1 0 0) (new 2 1 4) == new 3 1 4

    add zero one == one

values.name: clamp
values.comment:
Clamp a 3D vector between a min and max vector

    original = new 10 -50 2

    clamped =
        original
            |> clamp (new 0 0 0) (new 5 5 5)

    clamped == new 5 0 2

values.name: direction
values.comment:
Get the direction from one vector to another.

    playerPosition : Vector3
    playerPosition =
        new 3 4 5

    enemyPosition : Vector3
    enemyPosition =
        new 1 0 8

    playerToEnemy : Vector3
    playerToEnemy =
        direction
            { from = playerPosition
            , to = enemyPosition
            }

**Fun fact**: This is the same as _subtracting_ one vector from the other!

values.name: dot
values.comment:
Get the dot product between two vectors

    dot positiveX positiveX == 1

    dot positiveX negativeX == -1

    dot positiveX positiveY == 0

    dot positiveX (new 5 0 0) == 5

values.name: fromFloat
fromRecord
values.comment:
Create a vector from a record

    position : Vector3
    position =
        fromRecord { x = 3, y = 4, z = 5 }
values.type: { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float, z : Basics.Float } -> Elm3d.Vector3.Vector3

values.name: length
values.comment:
Get the length of the current vector

    length (new 1 0 0) == 1

    length (new 3 4 0) == 5

values.name: multiply
values.comment:
Multiply one vector by another. The resulting vector has the value `(x1 * x3, y1 * y3)`

    multiply (new 3 4 1) (new 1 1 1) == new 3 4 1

    multiply (new 1 1 1) (new 3 4 1) == new 3 4 1

    multiply (new 3 4 5) (new 1 0 0) == new 3 0 0

    multiply (new 3 4 5) (new 2 0 0) == new 6 0 0

    multiply zero one == zero

values.name: negativeX
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (-1, 0, 0)

    negativeX == fromRecord { x = -1, y = 0, z = 0 }

    negativeX == new -1 0 0

    negativeX == scale -1 positiveX

values.name: negativeY
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, -1, 0)

    negativeY == fromRecord { x = 0, y = -1, z = 0 }

    negativeY == new 0 -1 0

    negativeY == scale -1 positiveY

values.name: negativeZ
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, 0, -1)

    negativeZ == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0, z = -1 }

    negativeZ == new 0 0 -1

    negativeZ == scale -1 positiveZ

values.name: new
values.comment:
Create a vector from two floats

    position : Vector3
    position =
        new 3 4 5

values.name: normalize
values.comment:
A unit vector with the same direction as the given vector.

    normalize (new 1 0 0) == new 1 0 0

    normalize (new 9 0 0) == new 1 0 0

    normalize (new 1 1 1)
        == new
            (sqrt (1 / 3))
            (sqrt (1 / 3))
            (sqrt (1 / 3))

For convenience, normalizing a vector of length zero, returns the input vector:

    normalize (new 0 0 0) == new 0 0 0

values.name: one
values.comment:
Create the vector (1, 1, 1)

    one == fromRecord { x = 1, y = 1, z = 1 }

    one == new 1 1 1

    one == fromFloat 1

values.name: positiveX
positiveY
positiveZ
scale
values.comment:
Multiply the vector by a scalar value.

    scale 3 (new 1 1 1) == new 3 3 3

    scale 5 (new 1 1 1) == new 5 5 5

    scale 5 (new 3 0 0) == new 10 0 0

    scale 5 (new 3 4 5) == multiply (new 5 5 5) (new 3 4 5)

    scale 0 one == zero

values.name: toRecord
values.comment: Get the vector as an Elm record
values.type: Elm3d.Vector3.Vector3 -> { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float, z : Basics.Float }

values.name: x
values.comment: Get the x-coordinate for this vector

values.name: y
values.comment: Get the y-coordinate for this vector

values.name: z
values.comment: Get the z-coordinate for this vector

values.name: zero
values.comment:
Create the vector (0, 0, 0)

    zero == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0, z = 0 }

    zero == new 0 0 0

    zero == fromFloat 0

name: Elm3d.Vector4

values.name: add
values.comment:
Add two vectors together

    add (new 1 0 0 0) (new 2 1 4 5) == new 3 1 4 5

    add zero one == one

values.name: clamp
values.comment:
Clamp a 4D vector between a min and max vector

    original = new 10 -50 2 0

    clamped =
        original
            |> clamp (new 0 0 0 0) (new 5 5 5 5)

    clamped == new 5 0 2 0

values.name: direction
values.comment:
Get the direction from one vector to another.

    playerPosition : Vector4
    playerPosition =
        new 3 4 5 0

    enemyPosition : Vector4
    enemyPosition =
        new 1 0 8 0

    playerToEnemy : Vector4
    playerToEnemy =
        direction
            { from = playerPosition
            , to = enemyPosition
            }

**Fun fact**: This is the same as _subtracting_ one vector from the other!

values.name: dot
values.comment:
Get the dot product between two vectors

    dot positiveX positiveX == 1

    dot positiveX negativeX == -1

    dot positiveX positiveY == 0

    dot positiveX (new 5 0 0 0) == 5

values.name: fromFloat
values.comment:
Create a vector from a single float

    fromFloat 10 == new 10 10 10 10

    fromFloat 25
        == fromRecord
            { x = 25
            , y = 25
            , z = 25
            , w = 25
            }

values.name: fromRecord
values.comment:
Create a vector from a record

    position : Vector4
    position =
        fromRecord { x = 3, y = 4, z = 5, w = 6 }
values.type: { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float, z : Basics.Float, w : Basics.Float } -> Elm3d.Vector4.Vector4

values.name: length
values.comment:
Get the length of the current vector

    length (new 1 0 0 0) == 1

    length (new 3 4 0 0) == 5

values.name: multiply
values.comment:
Multiply one vector by another. The resulting vector has the value `(x1 * x3, y1 * y3)`

    multiply (new 3 4 1 0) (new 1 1 1 0) == new 3 4 1 0

    multiply (new 1 1 1 0) (new 3 4 1 0) == new 3 4 1 0

    multiply (new 3 4 5 0) (new 1 0 0 0) == new 3 0 0 0

    multiply (new 3 4 5 0) (new 2 0 0 0) == new 6 0 0 0

    multiply zero one == zero

values.name: negativeW
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, 0, 0, -1)

    negativeW == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0, z = 0, w = -1 }

    negativeW == new 0 0 0 -1

    negativeW == scale -1 positiveW

values.name: negativeX
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (-1, 0, 0, 0)

    negativeX == fromRecord { x = -1, y = 0, z = 0, w = 0 }

    negativeX == new -1 0 0 0

    negativeX == scale -1 positiveX

values.name: negativeY
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, -1, 0, 0)

    negativeY == fromRecord { x = 0, y = -1, z = 0, w = 0 }

    negativeY == new 0 -1 0 0

    negativeY == scale -1 positiveY

values.name: negativeZ
values.comment:
Create the unit vector (0, 0, -1, 0)

    negativeZ == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0, z = -1, w = 0 }

    negativeZ == new 0 0 -1 0

    negativeZ == scale -1 positiveZ

values.name: new
values.comment:
Create a vector from two floats

    position : Vector4
    position =
        new 3 4 5 6

values.name: normalize
values.comment:
A unit vector with the same direction as the given vector.

    normalize (new 1 0 0 0) == new 1 0 0 0

    normalize (new 9 0 0 0) == new 1 0 0 0

    normalize (new 1 1 1 1) == new 0.5 0.5 0.5 0.5

For convenience, normalizing a vector of length zero, returns the input vector:

    normalize (new 0 0 0 0) == new 0 0 0 0

values.name: one
values.comment:
Create the vector (1, 1, 1, 1)

    one == fromRecord { x = 1, y = 1, z = 1, w = 1 }

    one == new 1 1 1 1

    one == fromFloat 1

values.name: positiveW
positiveX
positiveY
positiveZ
scale
values.comment:
Multiply the vector by a scalar value.

    scale 3 (new 1 1 1 1) == new 3 3 3 3

    scale 5 (new 1 1 1 1) == new 5 5 5 5

    scale 5 (new 3 0 0 0) == new 10 0 0 0

    scale 5 (new 3 4 5 6) == multiply (new 5 5 5 5) (new 3 4 5 6)

    scale 0 one == zero

values.name: toRecord
values.comment: Get the vector as an Elm record
values.type: Elm3d.Vector4.Vector4 -> { x : Basics.Float, y : Basics.Float, z : Basics.Float, w : Basics.Float }

values.name: w
values.comment: Get the w-coordinate for this vector

values.name: x
values.comment: Get the x-coordinate for this vector

values.name: y
values.comment: Get the y-coordinate for this vector

values.name: z
values.comment: Get the z-coordinate for this vector

values.name: zero
values.comment:
Create the vector (0, 0, 0, 0)

    zero == fromRecord { x = 0, y = 0, z = 0, w = 0 }

    zero == new 0 0 0 0

    zero == fromFloat 0

name: Elm3d.Viewport

unions.name: Viewport
unions.comment: Represents how much space your 3D element should take up.

values.name: fixed
values.comment:
Regardless of window size, always be a fixed amount of pixels. If the window is smaller than that fixed size, users will see a scrollbar.

    toSize ( 1920, 1080 ) (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == ( 400, 300 )

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == ( 400, 300 )

    toSize ( 480, 480 ) (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == ( 400, 300 )

values.name: fullscreen
values.comment:
Always take up the entire window, without a fixed aspect ratio.

    toSize ( 1920, 1080 ) fullscreen
        == ( 1920, 1080 )

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) fullscreen
        == ( 800, 600 )

    toSize ( 480, 480 ) fullscreen
        == ( 480, 480 )

values.name: fullscreenAspect
values.comment:
Take up as much of the window as possible, but stay **centered on the page**
and **maintain the provided aspect ratio** (16:9, 4:3, etc).

    toSize ( 1920, 1080 ) (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == ( 1920, 1080 )

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == ( 800, 450 )

    toSize ( 480, 480 ) (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == ( 480, 180 )

values.name: isFullscreen
values.comment:
Return `True` if this is using `fullscreen`

    isFullscreen fullscreen
        == True

    isFullscreen (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == False

    isFullscreen (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == False

values.name: isFullscreenAspect
values.comment:
Return `True` if this is using `fullscreenAspect`

    isFullscreen fullscreen
        == False

    isFullscreen (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == True

    isFullscreen (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == False

values.name: toSize
values.comment:
When given the browser window's dimensions, this returns the size the rendered 3D viewport should be.

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) fullscreen
        == ( 800, 600 )

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) (fullscreenAspect (16 / 9))
        == ( 800, 450 )

    toSize ( 800, 600 ) (fixed ( 400, 300 ))
        == ( 400, 300 )
